meta.title: Build As You Speak: Applied Informatics for Clinical Research

glossary.definition: A small, embedded group of domain-fluent informaticists, research engineers, data stewards, and clinical subject-matter experts that operates inside an institution's security perimeter and partners directly with investigators to translate research questions into validated software.
Artificial Intelligence — in this context, large language models (LLMs) used as development assistants for code generation, design consultation, and review automation.
A defined group of patients or specimens selected for a research question by criteria such as diagnosis, treatment, biomarker status, or temporal window.
The process of finding the patients or specimens that match a research question — historically the slowest step in clinical research because it depends on querying multiple incompatible data sources.
Clinical Research Unit — an institution-level group that supports investigator-initiated and sponsored clinical research with regulatory, operational, and data infrastructure.
Clinical Trial Management System — software for managing the operational, financial, and regulatory aspects of a clinical trial.
Clinical and Translational Science Award — an NIH-funded program at U.S. academic medical centers that supports translational research infrastructure.
A person accountable for the quality, lineage, access policy, and appropriate use of a defined dataset within an institution.
A containerization platform that packages software with all its dependencies into portable containers, ensuring it runs identically on any machine.
Electronic Health Record — a digital version of a patient's medical chart, maintained by healthcare providers across encounters and settings.
A distributed, open-source search and analytics engine — widely used for log analytics, monitoring, and full-text search at enterprise scale.
A short-lived, task-scoped agreement with a model provider — established for the duration of a specific workload rather than as a persistent data-sharing relationship.
Extract, Transform, Load — the process of pulling data from one source, reshaping it, and depositing it into another. Most one-off research data pulls are unacknowledged ETL.
A workflow need that is technically attainable but absent from vendor software — the central concept from Gershkovich & Sinard (2015).
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act — U.S. federal law governing the privacy and security of protected health information (PHI).

Integrated Biospecimen and Informatics System — a natural-language search layer over pathology data built at Yale to make structured and unstructured clinical information accessible without writing queries.
Institutional Review Board — the body that reviews and approves research involving human subjects to ensure ethical conduct and regulatory compliance.
JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable data format used to store and exchange structured data between systems and applications.
Laboratory Information System — software that manages clinical laboratory operations including specimen tracking, test ordering, result reporting, and quality control.
Large Language Model — an AI system trained on vast text and code corpora, capable of generating natural language, writing code, and reasoning across domains.
Protected Health Information — any patient data covered by HIPAA privacy regulations.
Principal Investigator — the researcher leading and accountable for a study.
Rapidly Evolving Agile Laboratory Modules — the family of clinical and research informatics applications built inside Yale Pathology over nearly two decades.
Research Electronic Data Capture — a widely used, web-based platform for building and managing data collection instruments for clinical research.
The structural definition of a dataset — the fields, types, and relationships that determine what can and cannot be represented within it.
Subject-Matter Expert — a person with deep, often tacit, knowledge of a specific clinical or operational domain.
User Requirements Specification — a document that defines what a system must do from the user's perspective, written in plain language.

An open-source synthetic name generator that produces linguistically plausible names belonging to no real person — built for safe, HIPAA-compliant test data generation using syllabic mixing across seven languages.

slides.title: Build As You Speak
slides.subtitle: Applied Informatics for Clinical Research — from question to enterprise-grade tool, in weeks

slides.title: Conflict of Interest Disclosure
bullets: Consultant for Applicate Technologies Inc.
This presentation is intended solely for educational purposes. All content reflects the presenter's independent views and does not represent the official position of any institution or vendor.

slides.title: The Research Question That Quietly Died
slides.subtitle: Not because it was wrong — because the infrastructure said no

cards.label: The Cohort Question
cards.description: “How many patients with mismatch repair-deficient colon cancer received adjuvant therapy and recurred within 24 months?” — lived only in free-text reports.

cards.label: The Analyst Queue
cards.description: Pull request submitted; the cohort would be ready by the time the question was no longer interesting.

cards.label: The Schema Mismatch
cards.description: REDCap form built for the previous protocol; the new variable doesn’t fit. Add a column, add a year.

cards.label: The Different Question
cards.description: The question that finally got asked was the one the data could answer — not the one the science needed.

takeaway.text: Researchers have been shaping questions to fit infrastructure. That equation just inverted.
takeaway.comment: Don’t editorialize this slide. Read the four scenarios, pause, and let the room recognize their own questions. The recognition does the work for you.

slides.title: Thesis: The Metaphor Became Practical
slides.subtitle: “Software should be the immediate result of your thought” — once aspirational, now operational (Lincoln & Korpman, 1980)

left.heading: What Has Not Changed
bullets: Clinical research is built on logical sciences — representation, inference, decision, system behavior
The hardest research problems are semantic: cohort definition, time windows, provenance, missingness
These require domain expertise — the kind PIs and clinical fellows already have

right.heading: What AI Changed
bullets: Not what needs to be built — the economics of building it
The gap between “I know what this should do” and “it exists” collapsed
~100× reduction in effort to reach the same regulated quality bar

takeaway.text: The bottleneck was never the idea — it was the cost of implementation. That cost just dropped by two orders of magnitude.
takeaway.comment: Slow down on “immediate.” That’s the verb that just changed. Lincoln & Korpman wrote about clinical practice, but the principle generalizes: any domain expert with a precise question can now have a tool built to answer it.

slides.speakerNotes: Five objectives that build on each other for a research audience: (1) Identify a question in your own program that current infrastructure cannot answer in a reasonable timeframe. (2) Describe the research functionality gap and recognize workarounds as evidence rather than user failure. (3) Explain how AI changed the cost — not the quality bar — of building research tooling. (4) Distinguish a vendor product, a service center, and an applied informatics team — and recognize which is the right partner for which question. (5) Draft a one-page specification for a tool you wish existed in your research workflow. The visualization on screen shows the underlying progression — from understanding to action — that all five objectives share.

slides.title: Who This Talk Is For
slides.subtitle: Look around the room — if any of these describe you, you are exactly the audience
bullets: The PI with a question that didn’t survive the analyst queue — the cohort that would have been ready six months too late.
The fellow who learned to want the answerable question — the one the data could support, not the one the science needed.
The investigator who keeps a personal spreadsheet — the shadow database that holds what the official system can’t represent.
The researcher who has built a thing or two — and has never been told that what they did was a legitimate scientific act.
The leader who funds research infrastructure — and suspects the current operating model is not the right one for the next decade.

takeaway.text: If you are any of these, the rest of this talk is addressed to you specifically.
takeaway.comment: Look up from the slides for this one. This is the slide where you tell the room they belong here. The next 40 minutes lands harder once they’ve heard themselves named.

slides.title: Every Research Action Is an Information Event
slides.subtitle: The work is scientific — the optimization is informatics

cards.label: Cohort Definition
cards.description: Who counts — inclusion/exclusion criteria expressed against a real, messy, multi-source data substrate

cards.label: Data Extraction
cards.description: Where each value came from, when, under what definition — the audit trail your reviewers will ask for

cards.label: Harmonization
cards.description: Codes vs. text vs. units vs. eras — making variables comparable across time and source

cards.label: Analysis & Iteration
cards.description: Re-pull, re-define, re-cut. The first cohort is almost never the final cohort.

takeaway.text: The science is inseparable from information flow. AI makes the optimization newly affordable; informatics makes it correct.
takeaway.comment: You don’t “do informatics” by clicking buttons in REDCap. You face informatics problems whenever you have to reason about identity, meaning, provenance, and downstream consequences before you can analyze. Informatics is how we redesign the flow so the system carries more of the cognitive burden.

slides.title: The Research Functionality Gap
slides.subtitle: Vendor stack vs. your specific question — where they don’t meet (Gershkovich & Sinard, 2015, applied to research)

left.heading: What the Vendor Stack Was Designed For
bullets: EHR — billing, compliance, encounter documentation
LIS — specimen tracking, result reporting
REDCap — forms-based data capture for the protocol you anticipated
CTMS — trial operations, regulatory binders, finance
Data warehouse — the queries someone else wrote for someone else

right.heading: What Your Question Actually Needs
bullets: Cohort discovery over both structured and free-text data
Variables the protocol didn’t exist to capture two years ago
Joins across systems that were never designed to talk
Iteration — the third cohort, not the first, is the one you analyze
Provenance a reviewer can audit

takeaway.text: Vendor research infrastructure solves the general case. Your study is a specific case. The gap between them is where workarounds live — and where errors do.
takeaway.comment: This is not anti-vendor. The general-purpose research stack is essential. But it cannot anticipate your study’s specifics, and the moment you try to bend it to the specific case, the friction starts. That friction is the gap.

slides.title: Anatomy of a Cohort Delay
slides.subtitle: Where the model breaks — step-level granularity, not phase-level labels

steps.label: Question
steps.detail: Investigator formulates the cohort question. The expensive part starts here.

steps.label: IRB
steps.detail: Protocol amendment or exempt-determination request. Necessary; not on your critical path.

steps.label: Pull request
steps.detail: Submitted to data warehouse / honest broker / cancer registry. The clock starts.

steps.label: Analyst queue
steps.delayNote: 10–16 weeks is typical — not the analyst's fault, the queue depth
steps.detail: Your request joins everyone else's. By the time it surfaces, your science has moved.

steps.label: First pull
steps.detail: Initial cohort returned. Almost always missing a variable, capturing the wrong era, or counting the wrong denominator.

steps.label: Harmonization
steps.detail: Codes vs. text vs. units vs. era. The unacknowledged ETL inside every research project.

steps.label: Re-pull
steps.delayNote: Definition refined — back to the queue
steps.detail: The first cohort wasn’t the question — it was the question made operational. The refinement triggers a second wait.

steps.label: Second pull
steps.detail: Smaller delta this time, but still measured in weeks.

steps.label: Analysis
steps.detail: The intellectual work — modest in clock time relative to everything that preceded it.

steps.label: Reviewer ask
steps.delayNote: “Re-stratify by…” — back to the queue again
steps.detail: The reviewer asks one new variable. You return to the analyst queue. Three more months.

takeaway.text: Calling this “data access friction” tells you nothing. The delay lives in specific handoffs between specific steps — and the fix depends entirely on which one.
takeaway.comment: Was it the queue? The harmonization? The re-pull triggered by reviewer feedback? Each demands a different intervention. You need step-level granularity, not the phrase “data isn’t accessible.”

slides.title: The Research Workarounds You Already Use
slides.subtitle: Each one is system-design evidence — not user failure

cards.label: Personal Spreadsheets
cards.description: The investigator’s private database holding what the official system can’t represent

cards.label: Manual Chart Review
cards.description: Fellows reading PDFs to extract a variable a database should have computed

cards.label: “Ask the Analyst”
cards.description: A person becoming the bus between systems that were never designed to talk

cards.label: Shared Drive Forks
cards.description: Five copies of “final_v3_USE_THIS” — nobody can reconstruct which was the analysis dataset

cards.label: Sticky Notes
cards.description: Monitors decorated with the workflow logic the system was supposed to encode

takeaway.text: Workarounds are not user failure — they are requirements documents written in frustration.
takeaway.comment: Catalog them in your group. Every spreadsheet, every shared-drive fork, every “ask Carla” ritual is a feature request your infrastructure didn’t hear. The list will be longer than you expect, and it will be the cleanest backlog you’ve ever generated.

slides.title: The Governing Principle
slides.subtitle: “Software should be the immediate result of your thought” — the title of this lecture is also its thesis

takeaway.text: Build as you speak. The earliest clinical and research systems came from exactly this principle — pioneers automating their own work, not someone else’s idea of their work.
takeaway.comment: Lincoln, Korpman, Lindberg, Greenes — the founders of medical informatics built systems for their own questions. Then the field professionalized, software got expensive, and researchers were displaced from their own pipelines. AI restored access to the original mode. The applied team is how we stay there.

slides.title: Poll: What Blocks Your Research Most?
slides.prompt: Which of these is the biggest source of friction in your current research program?
options: Cohort discovery / accessing the right patients
Schema mismatch (variable doesn’t fit the form)
Joining data across systems that don’t talk
Synthetic / test data for pipeline development
slides.note: We will return to your answers when we discuss what the new operating model makes possible.

slides.title: When You Hit a Gap: The Seven Options
slides.subtitle: From Gershkovich & Sinard (2015) — the same decision framework, applied to research

takeaway.text: Most research programs default to options 1–3 (ignore, work around, spreadsheet) for the same reason most labs do: 5–7 used to be unaffordable. They aren’t anymore.
takeaway.comment: Ignoring the gap or patching manually is the path of least resistance — but every workaround is a future error and a future delay. AI-assisted development just made options 5–7 (custom build, applied team, AI-assisted) accessible to research programs that never had the budget for them.

slides.title: Two Orders of Magnitude
slides.subtitle: What AI changed is not what we build or how we review it — it is the cost of satisfying quality standards

cards.label: Quality Gates
cards.description: IRB, validation, traceability, audit trail, data stewardship — same rigor as before

cards.label: Effort Reduction
cards.description: Work that took a research engineering team months now takes one informaticist weeks

cards.label: Supplementary Tools
cards.description: The small infrastructure pieces you used to wish for and never got — now emerge naturally

cards.label: Foundation
cards.description: AI amplified a methodology that already worked — build the discipline before the tooling

takeaway.text: Same gates. Same quality bar. Hundred times less effort. That is the transformation.
takeaway.comment: AI did not create our methodology. It amplified a methodology that already worked. Organizations that try to use AI without the underlying discipline will just produce bugs faster. The sequencing matters — build the culture first.

slides.title: Cognitive Load Redistribution
slides.subtitle: Where research-engineering effort concentrates — the structural shift

takeaway.text: Freeing up cognitive load is only valuable if you have something worth thinking about.
takeaway.comment: Every time a research engineer looks up syntax for the fortieth time, that is not thinking. AI absorbs it. What remains — what should remain — is the question of whether you’re building the right thing at all. For PIs, that question is the one you’re uniquely qualified to answer.

slides.title: Compression of the Feedback Loop
slides.subtitle: Speed change is not linear — it changes the character of design decisions

takeaway.text: Speed doesn’t just change how fast you work. It changes how you think.
takeaway.comment: When a cohort definition takes two weeks to test, you commit to fewer of them. Compress the loop and the economics change. You can prototype three definitions before lunch and pick the one that actually answers the question.

slides.speakerNotes: AI accelerates implementation. AI does not clarify intent. The decision about what to build — the right cohort definition, the right variable model, the right inclusion logic — is unchanged. Vague requirements now produce working code that does the wrong thing. In research: if you can’t precisely define what “mismatch repair-deficient with adjuvant therapy and 24-month recurrence” means in your data substrate, AI will build you a beautiful pipeline that gets it wrong. Precision of thought is no longer optional. The fork on this slide is real: clear intent produces the right system at scale; vague intent produces working code for the wrong question, faster than ever.

AI can use codified knowledge: rules, dictionaries, ontologies, schema specifications. It cannot inherit lived experience, tacit clinical judgment, or institutional memory. What matters in clinical research is often knowing which ambiguity is dangerous, which exception matters, and when a formally correct cohort is operationally wrong. The PI with deep clinical knowledge and AI assistance is not slightly better than one without AI — the gap is qualitative. In your research, you ARE the domain expert. The edge cases in your protocol, the clinical context that determines whether a count is meaningful, the institutional history that explains why a code was used differently before 2018 — that knowledge is yours. AI handles the implementation. You provide the judgment.

slides.title: IBIS — Natural-Language Cohort Discovery
slides.subtitle: What it does, and why it matters for the question you almost stopped asking

left.heading: What It Does
bullets: Investigator asks a natural-language question — no SQL, no analyst queue
IBIS turns it into a query over both structured and unstructured pathology data
Returns a cohort in seconds, not weeks
Iterate the definition live — refine, re-run, refine again, in the same sitting

right.heading: How It Was Built
bullets: Built outside Yale on entirely synthetic data
Docker-based Elasticsearch stack — never touched PHI
Pathway in: open source → adapt to internal data model → Enterprise GitHub → security review → CI/CD
AI-assisted development made this feasible for one informaticist

takeaway.text: The previous-team-sized project just became a one-informaticist project. The bottleneck moved from engineering capacity to question precision.
takeaway.comment: This is the existence proof for cohort discovery. The methodology, the people, the discipline — already existed. What changed was the cost of building, which made the project feasible at the scale of one informaticist plus an applied team for safety review.

slides.speakerNotes: This is the architecture diagram. The top half is what the investigator does — asks a natural-language question, IBIS turns it into a query over structured and unstructured pathology data, returns a cohort. Seconds, not weeks. The bottom half is the part the room needs to see clearly. IBIS was built outside the institution, on entirely synthetic data, in a Docker-based Elasticsearch stack. Everything you’re about to see runs on a machine that has never touched PHI. The pathway in is the responsible one — bring it in as open source, adapt to the internal data model, Enterprise GitHub, security review, CI/CD. That’s the route, and it works. If the live demo is available, switch to it now and run two queries: one structured (cohort by IHC profile or stage), one unstructured (a free-text concept that doesn’t have a code). After each query, one short sentence: ‘Notice — no SQL was written.’ / ‘Notice — that pulled from a free-text report, not a coded field.’ If the live demo is unavailable, switch to the recorded fallback without apologizing. The talk works without it; the talk does not work without the idea of it.

slides.title: What This Means for Your Research
slides.subtitle: Cohort discovery as an investigator-led act — not an analyst queue
bullets: The cohort definition becomes part of the science — you can refine it the way you refine a hypothesis, not the way you submit a ticket.
Free-text variables stop being trapped — the diagnostic clause in a report is now queryable evidence, not a PDF you ask a fellow to review.
Reviewer asks become a half-hour exercise — not a re-pull. “Can you re-stratify by…” becomes a query, run live.
The clock between question and answer collapses — from weeks to a working session.
The intellectual loop closes — question, data, hypothesis, refinement, all on the same time scale your thinking actually moves at.

takeaway.text: When the loop is short enough, your question shapes the data instead of the data shaping your question.
takeaway.comment: This is the most important slide in Act 4 for a research audience. The previous thirty years trained PIs to ask the answerable question. IBIS — and tools like it — expand what ‘answerable’ means.

slides.title: Xenonym — Names That Don’t Exist
slides.subtitle: Case Study — the supplementary tool that was never worth building before · github.com/OpenPathologyFoundation/xenonym

left.heading: The Research Problem
bullets: You are building a research data pipeline that handles patient data
You need realistic test data that exercises schema, validation, and integration
You cannot use real patients — HIPAA is not a suggestion, IRB is not a suggestion
Obviously fake data (John Doe) tests almost nothing — your pipeline will look correct and break in production

right.heading: The Requirement
bullets: Names that sound like names but belong to no one
Names that break systems — NULL, DROP, diacritics
Names from diverse linguistic origins
A tool that was never worth building before AI collapsed the cost

takeaway.text: Obviously fake data tests almost nothing. Real data is irregular, surprising, and occasionally adversarial.
takeaway.comment: The boring solution is John Doe and Jane Smith. But that tests almost nothing, because real data is irregular, surprising, and adversarial. The point of Xenonym is not the tool. The point is that supplementary research infrastructure of this kind is now affordable to build.

slides.speakerNotes: The design challenge: how do you generate names that feel linguistically plausible but are structurally foreign to any single language? The solution AI surfaced: syllabic mixing across seven languages — Finnish, Georgian, Yoruba, Japanese, Welsh, Swahili, Hungarian. The probability that the combination is a real name is vanishingly small. The probability that it sounds like a name to a human ear is high. I did not search for this solution. I described the constraint and the model surfaced an approach from computational linguistics that I would not have reached independently. This is AI functioning as a cross-domain consultant — the mode that matters most for clinical research, where our problems span medicine, computer science, statistics, and operations.

slides.title: Xenonym Live — Schema-Driven Synthetic Data for Research
slides.subtitle: Live at xenonym.openpathology.org

takeaway.text: Publicly accessible. Costs nothing. Built to solve a real research-pipeline problem — not to demonstrate a capability.
takeaway.comment: Xenonym generates HIPAA-safe synthetic patient data including adversarial edge cases: names containing NULL, DROP, DELETE (SQL injection test cases), diacritics (Müller, Čapek, Ñoño). If your research pipeline can’t store ‘Nontra Null,’ that’s a bug — and depending on context, a compliance failure waiting to happen.

slides.title: What IBIS and Xenonym Have in Common
slides.subtitle: The shared trait neither tool would have existed without
bullets: Neither was economically rational before. The effort to build either one used to exceed the rational return for any single research program.
Both were built by one informaticist with AI assistance, against synthetic data, then brought inside the institution along the responsible pathway.
Both solve a real, persistent research problem — not a theoretical one, not a demo problem.
Both are open source. The methodology is reproducible at any institution willing to fund a small applied team.
Both presuppose precise specification. The tools that emerged from vague intent would have been worse than the absence of tools.

takeaway.text: The supplementary infrastructure researchers used to wish for and never got — it is now within reach. The constraint is not the budget. The constraint is the team and the spec.
takeaway.comment: Tie the two artifacts together with this line and move on. Don’t over-claim. The point of the two examples is that they are real and they exist; the rest of the talk is the operating model that lets the next two exist.

slides.title: The Missing Layer in Most Institutions
slides.subtitle: You have IT. You have researchers. You don’t have the layer between them.
bullets: IT keeps the lights on — networks, identity, the EHR upgrade. Necessary; not designed to build for your study.
Researchers have questions and clinical context — but rent the pipeline from people who can’t hear the question precisely.
Central analyst service centers take orders. They process queues. They do not co-design.
Software vendors sell features. They cannot build the specific thing that would answer your specific question.
Between IT and the researcher, there should be an applied informatics team. Most institutions do not have one. The ones that do, build.

takeaway.text: The applied informatics team is the operating model that makes build-as-you-speak safe and sustainable.
takeaway.comment: This is the framing slide for Act 5. Slow down. The next four slides explain who is on the team, what they do, and how it differs from the alternatives the room may already be paying for.

slides.title: Anatomy of an Applied Informatics Team
slides.subtitle: Small. Embedded. Inside the security perimeter. 4–8 people, not 40.

cards.label: Domain-Fluent Informaticist
cards.description: Reads clinical context and engineering at the same time — turns a PI’s question into a precise specification

cards.label: Research Engineer
cards.description: Implements against the spec — with AI as an amplifier, not a replacement

cards.label: Data Steward
cards.description: Owns lineage, access policy, and the boundary between synthetic and real data

cards.label: Clinical SME
cards.description: PI, fellow, or pathologist embedded in the team — the one who knows when a result is operationally wrong

cards.label: QA & Validation
cards.description: Owns the test suite, the regression policy, the audit trail — part-time at small scale, full role at larger ones

takeaway.text: The right size is roughly the size of a research lab. Larger and it becomes a service center. Smaller and it can’t carry validation.
takeaway.comment: Be specific in the room. Name the roles. Give a number. The applied team is recognizable to a researcher because it looks like a research lab — a small group of people with overlapping skills, working on a focused set of problems, accountable to each other rather than to a queue.

slides.title: Three Operating Models, Only One of Which Co-Designs

left.heading: What Service Centers and Vendors Do
bullets: Service center: takes orders, processes queues, returns deliverables
Vendor: sells features, defines the workflow, controls the roadmap
Both treat the question as fixed input
Neither asks “what would actually answer this?” before building
Both are essential. Neither is sufficient.

right.heading: What an Applied Team Does
bullets: Hears the question — then sharpens it with the researcher
Co-designs the specification before any code is written
Builds inside the institution’s security perimeter
Validates against the researcher’s edge cases
Owns the artifact long-term — fixes, extends, retires

takeaway.text: An applied team is the only operating model that asks your question back to you and helps you sharpen it before it builds anything.
takeaway.comment: Three columns in the speaker’s head, two on the slide for clarity. The applied team is the one that says no — ‘that’s not quite the right question yet, let’s sharpen it before we build.’ A service center can’t say that. A vendor won’t.

slides.title: What an Applied Team Produces — Nineteen Years of Evidence
slides.subtitle: REALM: Rapidly Evolving Agile Laboratory Modules — the operational landscape one applied team has built inside one institution

takeaway.text: This is what one applied team has produced inside one academic department. The point is not that we did it. The point is that the operating model works — and AI just made it dramatically more accessible.
takeaway.comment: If anyone asks whether this is reproducible: yes. The methodology is open. The team size is modest. The institutional pattern — Enterprise GitHub, security review, CI/CD — exists at most academic medical centers. What the talk is asking the room to do is not to build a copy of REALM. It’s to start the team that, in nineteen years, will have its own.

slides.title: The Safety Boundary in a Research Pipeline

left.heading: AI Does (Development Partner)
bullets: Generate UI scaffold and pipeline boilerplate from specs
Write event handlers, state management, ETL stubs
Draft test skeletons from research requirements
Surface cross-domain techniques (e.g., computational linguistics for synthetic data)
Debug, refactor, and fix tooling issues

right.heading: AI Must NOT (Non-Negotiable)
bullets: No autonomous research conclusions — AI assists pipeline construction, humans interpret results
No PHI in prompts — patient data never enters AI context
No hidden transformations — every cohort filter, every join, every recode must be testable
No unverified production code — tested and traced before any data moves through it

takeaway.text: AI is a development partner, not a research participant. The safety envelope is locked by humans plus automated tests — never by the model alone.
takeaway.comment: Read both columns aloud. The non-negotiables matter as much as the capabilities, especially for a research audience that may be hearing the AI argument for the first time. Every AI risk has a testable mitigation: hallucinated logic → unit tests; prompt drift → regression suites; data leakage → no-PHI policy enforced structurally; supply-chain risk → minimal dependencies.

slides.title: Governance: The Same Framework, Extended
slides.subtitle: AI governance for research is the natural extension of data stewardship — not a separate discipline

left.heading: The Questions
bullets: Validation: who certifies the pipeline did what it was specified to do?
Provenance: can a reviewer reconstruct how each value was derived?
Bias: what training-data composition affects performance for your subpopulations?
Documentation: when AI contributed to the pipeline, what goes in the methods section?

right.heading: The Principles
bullets: Stewardship extends: AI governance is the natural extension of research data stewardship you already do
Same framework: requirements → tests → traceability applies to AI-built tools too
Ephemeral provider contracts: short, task-scoped agreements with model providers — not persistent data-sharing relationships
Vendor claims ≠ institutional validation: verify independently

takeaway.text: You already know how to do this. The discipline that governs your data is the same discipline that governs the pipeline AI helped build.
takeaway.comment: This is the line that should reassure the room. The applied team is not asking the institution to invent a new governance regime. It is asking the institution to extend the regime that already exists — the one that governs your data warehouse, your IRB documents, your CTSA infrastructure — to the AI-assisted artifacts that will increasingly be part of how research is done.

epigraph.text: You don’t need to be a developer. You need to be the person who understands the question.

steps.question: Spot a gap
steps.detail: Identify one workaround in your research — a spreadsheet, a manual chart review, a re-pull cycle.

steps.question: Write the spec
steps.detail: One page. Five requirements with clinical rationale. Three plausible failure modes. Highest-leverage step.

steps.question: Find the team
steps.detail: Connect with your applied informatics team — or recruit the first informaticist who can become one.

steps.question: Validate
steps.detail: Be the clinical validator — your adversarial edge cases are gold, and only you can generate them.

steps.question: Advocate
steps.detail: Present the gap, the spec, the artifact, and the evidence to leadership. The next study after yours benefits.

takeaway.text: The new superpower: a one-page spec, an applied team, and a tool that ships in weeks instead of years.
takeaway.comment: The bottleneck is no longer coding — it’s domain clarity. And that’s what you already have.

slides.speakerNotes: Five actions arranged on a commitment timeline. Today: spot a gap. This week: write the spec. This month: find the team (or be the catalyst that forms one). This quarter: validate. This year: advocate. Walk it left to right. Make it sound small — because each individual step is. The compound effect is what changes the institution.

takeaway.text: The new superpower: a one-page spec, an applied team, and a tool that ships in weeks instead of years.
takeaway.comment: The bottleneck is no longer coding — it’s domain clarity. And that’s what you already have.

slides.title: What a One-Page Research Spec Looks Like
slides.subtitle: URS reframed for a research artifact — the document worth more than the code that follows it

left.heading: What Goes In It
bullets: The question — one sentence, sharp enough to answer with a yes or a number
5–10 requirements — each with a clinical rationale: why the system must do this
3–5 failure modes — the adversarial cases only the PI can imagine
Boundary conditions — what the tool must NOT do (no PHI in prompts, no autonomous decisions)
Acceptance test — how you and the team will know it works

right.heading: What It Replaces
bullets: The 30-page protocol document nobody reads
The vendor RFP nobody answers usefully
The standing meeting with central analytics that never quite resolves
The shadow spreadsheet — made obsolete by the artifact the spec produces
The compromise question — the one you asked because the data could answer it

takeaway.text: The spec is the deliverable. Once it exists, the build is tractable. Without it, no amount of AI helps.
takeaway.comment: Show what one looks like in your own practice if you have a clean example. The example matters more than the template. The point is that the spec is short, that it requires only domain expertise (which the room has), and that it is the highest-leverage thing the researcher can do this week.

slides.title: The Question Returns
bullets:
The question was never
“What can AI build for my research?”

The question is
“What research workflow do you understand deeply enough
to describe precisely — and recognize when it’s solved?”

takeaway.text: The clarity is the work.
takeaway.comment: If the answer is yes — if you have a question you can describe — then everything in this talk is an accelerant. The tools exist. The methodology exists. The cost barrier is gone. What remains is your expertise and the team to partner with.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: Researchers spent thirty years shaping questions to fit infrastructure. That equation just inverted — you can now shape infrastructure to fit your question.
The research functionality gap is real: the vendor stack solves the general case; your study is a specific case. Workarounds are evidence, not failure.
AI collapsed the cost of building by ~100×. Same IRB, same validation, same audit trail — radically less effort.
The new operating model is the applied informatics team — small, embedded, inside the security perimeter. The lone PI with an LLM is not the model.
The evidence is concrete: IBIS for cohort discovery, Xenonym for synthetic test data, and the principle they share — build as you speak.

cta.label: Your challenge:
cta.text: Identify one research workflow gap in your current program. Write a one-page spec with five requirements — each with a clinical rationale — plus three plausible failure modes. Bring it to your applied informatics team. If you don’t have one, that one-page document is also the strongest case you can make for funding one.
